    /**
     * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch.
     *
     * If the timestamp type is create time, this is the max timestamp among all records contained in this batch and
     * the value is updated during compaction.
     *
     * @return The max timestamp
     */
    long maxTimestamp();
After
    /**
     * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch.
     *
     * If the timestamp type is create time ({@link TimestampType#CREATE_TIME}), this is the max
     * timestamp among all records contained in this batch and the value is updated during log compaction.
     * If the timestamp type is log append time, this value is set by the broker.
     *
     * @return The max timestamp in milliseconds
     */
    long maxTimestamp();
8. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
After
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator which delays decompression of the record stream until the records are
     * actually requested using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the record format does not support streaming
     * iteration, the normal iterator is returned instead.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance characteristics:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>For uncompressed batches: Returns a minimal iterator with no decompression overhead
     *   <li>For compressed batches: Decompresses data on-demand as records are consumed, which can
     *       significantly reduce memory usage for large batches
     *   <li>Decompression buffers are obtained from the supplier, allowing buffer reuse for performance
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>Resource management:</strong> The returned iterator must be properly closed using
     * try-with-resources or explicit {@link CloseableIterator#close()} to release decompression resources.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating. A supplier that
     *                                    reuses buffers will have significant performance impact.
     * @return A closeable iterator over records in this batch. The iterator must be closed.
     * @see BufferSupplier#create() for creating a new non-reusing supplier
     * @see BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING for a caching-disabled supplier
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
9. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence e
... (truncated)
After
/**
 * {@link RecordBatch} implementation for magic version 2 and above. This class encapsulates the
 * binary serialization format of a Kafka record batch, including the batch header and all contained
 * records.
 *
 * <p><strong>Binary Format:</strong> The batch follows the RecordBatch schema:
 * <pre>
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 * </pre>
 * When compression is enabled, the compressed record data follows immediately after the record count.
 *
 * <p><strong>CRC Computation:</strong> The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used and covers all
 * bytes from the attributes field through the end of the batch. The partition leader epoch field is
 * excluded from CRC computation to avoid recomputation when the broker assigns a leader epoch.
 *
 * <p><strong>Log Compaction:</strong> Unlike older message formats, magic v2 batches preserve the
 * first and last offset/sequence numbers from the original batch during log compaction. This preservation
 * is critical for: (1) restoring producer state on broker restart, and (2) duplicate detection via
 * sequence number verification. Empty batches may be retained to preserve producer sequence information
 * until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires.
 *
 * <p><strong>Timestamp Semantics:</strong>
 * <ul>
 *   <li>BaseTimestamp: For CREATE_TIME, reflects the first record's timestamp. After compaction,
 *       reflects the first remaining record. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, set by the broker. I
... (truncated)

    private final ByteBuffer buffer;

    DefaultRecordBatch(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }

    @Override
    public byte magic() {
        return buffer.get(MAGIC_OFFSET);
    }

    @Override
    public void ensureValid() {
After
    private final ByteBuffer buffer;

    /**
     * Constructs a DefaultRecordBatch from a ByteBuffer. The buffer is expected to contain
     * a complete batch with valid header and record data. The buffer position and limit define
     * the boundaries of the batch data.
     *
     * <p><strong>Note:</strong> The batch maintains a reference to the provided buffer. Callers
     * should ensure the buffer remains valid and unmodified for the lifetime of the batch.
     *
     * @param buffer A ByteBuffer containing the serialized batch data. Must contain at least
     *               {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes.
     */
    DefaultRecordBatch(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }

    @Override
    public byte magic() {
        return buffer.get(MAGIC_OFFSET);
    }

    /**
     * Validates the integrity of this record batch. Checks that the batch size is sufficient
     * to contain at least the batch header overhead, and that the CRC checksum is correct.
     *
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if the batch is smaller than the required overhead or
     *                                if the stored CRC does not match the computed CRC
     */
    @Override
    public void ensureValid() {
11. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas.
     *
     * @return The base timestamp
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
After
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used as the reference for calculating
     * relative record timestamps (timestamp deltas). The interpretation of this value depends
     * on the timestamp type and whether the delete horizon flag is set.
     *
     * <p><strong>Semantics:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>If delete horizon is set: Contains the delete horizon timestamp for cleanup</li>
     *   <li>If CREATE_TIME: Timestamp of the first record in the batch (or first after compaction)</li>
     *   <li>If LOG_APPEND_TIME: Set by broker when batch is appended to log</li>
     *   <li>If batch is empty: Set to {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP}</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @return The base timestamp in milliseconds
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
12. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        buffer.put(this.buffer.duplicate());
    }

    @Override
    public void writeTo(ByteBufferOutputStream outputStream) {
        outputStream.write(this.buffer.duplicate());
    }
After
    /**
     * Writes this record batch to a ByteBuffer. Copies the entire batch including header and
     * all records to the provided buffer at its current position. The buffer's position is
     * updated by the number of bytes written.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance note:</strong> This method performs a bulk copy of the underlying
     * ByteBuffer slice, which is efficient.
     *
     * @param buffer The target ByteBuffer to write to. Must have at least {@link #sizeInBytes()}
     *               remaining space.
     * @throws java.nio.BufferOverflowException if the buffer lacks sufficient remaining space
     */
    @Override
    public void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        buffer.put(this.buffer.duplicate());
    }

    @Override
    public void writeTo(ByteBufferOutputStream outputStream) {
        outputStream.write(this.buffer.duplicate());
    }
13. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {
        if (count() == 0)
            return Collections.emptyIterator();

        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,
        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint
        // can use `streamingIterator` at the cost of additional complexity
        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iterator = compressedIterator(BufferSupplier.NO_CACHING, false)) {
            List<Record> records = new ArrayList<>(count());
            while (iterator.hasNext())
                records.add(iterator.next());
            return records.iterator();
        }
    }

    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
After
    /**
     * Returns an iterator over the records contained in this batch. For compressed batches,
     * the full batch is decompressed into memory. For efficiency with large compressed batches
     * that don't require holding all records in memory, use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance characteristics:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Uncompressed: Minimal memory overhead, direct buffer reads</li>
     *   <li>Compressed: Full batch decompressed into memory upfront; use streamingIterator
     *       for streaming decompression if memory is a concern</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @return An Iterator over records. Never null; may return an empty iterator if the batch
     *         contains zero records.
     */
    @Override
    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {
        if (count() == 0)
            return Collections.emptyIterator();

        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,
        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint
        // can use `streamingIterator` at the cost of additional complexity
        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iterator = compressedIterator(BufferSupplier.NO_CACHING, false)) {
            List<Record> records = new ArrayList<>(count());
            while (iterator.hasNext())
                records.add(iterator.next());
            return records.iterator();
        }
    }

    /**
     * Returns an iterator that skips key, value, and header data within records. This is useful
     * for validation and metrics collection where only record offset and timestamp are needed.
     *
     * <p><strong>Perfor
... (truncated)
14. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (isCompressed())
            return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false);
        else
            return uncompressedIterator();
    }
After
    /**
     * Returns a streaming iterator for records in this batch with deferred decompression.
     * For uncompressed batches, provides minimal overhead. For compressed batches, decompresses
     * data on-demand as records are consumed, reducing memory footprint compared to full upfront
     * decompression.
     *
     * <p><strong>Resource management:</strong> The returned iterator MUST be closed using
     * try-with-resources or explicit close() to properly release decompression resources.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance notes:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>For large compressed batches, this method provides significant memory savings by avoiding
     *       full decompression until records are actually read</li>
     *   <li>The decompression buffer supplier significantly impacts performance; reusing buffers
     *       across multiple streaming iterators can be more efficient than allocating fresh buffers</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier Supplies decompression buffers. For optimal performance with multiple
     *                       iterations, provide a reusing supplier via {@link BufferSupplier} methods.
     * @return A closeable iterator that must be closed. Never null.
     * @throws KafkaException if decompression fails
     */
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (isCompressed())
            return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false);
        else
            return uncompressedIterator();
    }
15. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
/**
 * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used only for reading or
 * modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer see {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder},
 * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)} variants.
 */
public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {
After
/**
 * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer for reading and in-place modification
 * of existing record batch data. This class provides memory-efficient access to serialized Kafka
 * record batches without requiring disk I/O.
 *
 * <p><strong>Purpose:</strong> MemoryRecords is used for three primary scenarios:
 * <ul>
 *   <li><strong>Reading:</strong> Deserializing record batches from a ByteBuffer (e.g., from fetch responses)</li>
 *   <li><strong>In-place modification:</strong> Updating batch metadata (timestamps, offsets) without copying</li>
 *   <li><strong>Filtering:</strong> Removing records during log compaction via {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)}</li>
 * </ul>
 *
 * <p><strong>Creating instances:</strong> For reading, use {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)}.
 * To create new batches, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or one of the static factory methods
 * like {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}.
 *
 * <p><strong>Thread-safety:</strong> {@code MemoryRecords} is <strong>not thread-safe</strong> for
 * mutation operations like filtering or in-place batch updates. The underlying ByteBuffer is mutable
 * and modifications via batch methods (e.g., {@link DefaultRecordBatch#setLastOffset(long)}) directly
 * modify the shared buffer. Multiple threads must not concurrently:
 * <ul>
 *   <li>Call {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)}</li>
 *   <li>Modify batches obtained via {@link #batches()}</li>
 *   <li>Modify or read the underlying buffer while another thread is modifying it</li>
 * </ul>
 *
 * Reading batches and iterating records <em>is</em> thread-safe if the underlying buffer is not
 * concurrently modified. Different instances of MemoryRecords can be safely accessed in paralle
... (truncated)
16. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * The total number of bytes in this message set not including any partial, trailing messages. This
     * may be smaller than what is returned by {@link #sizeInBytes()}.
     * @return The number of valid bytes
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
After
    /**
     * Returns the total number of bytes in this message set that form complete, valid batches.
     * This may be smaller than {@link #sizeInBytes()} if there are partial (incomplete) batches
     * at the end of the buffer.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance:</strong> O(n) where n is the number of batches. The result is cached
     * after the first call. Subsequent calls return the cached value without iteration.
     *
     * @return The number of valid bytes covering only complete batches
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
17. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
After
    /**
     * Filters records from this MemoryRecords into a destination ByteBuffer based on a filter function.
     * This method is used during log compaction to remove expired tombstones and aborted transaction markers.
     *
     * <p><strong>Filter Logic:</strong> For each batch:
     * <ol>
     *   <li>{@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} is called to determine if the batch
     *       should be completely deleted, retained empty, or checked record-by-record</li>
     *   <li>For retained batches, {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)} is called
     *       for each record to determine inclusion</li>
     *   <li>Filtered batches are written with updated metadata (timestamps, offsets, delete horizon)</li>
     * </ol>
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance characteristics:</strong>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>O(n) where n is the total number of records</li>
     *   <li>Compressed batches are decompressed using the supplied BufferSupplier; buffer reuse
     *       significantly improves performance on large compressed batches</li>
     *   <li>Memory: Output buffer may be reallocated if filtered data exceeds destination buffer capacity</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><strong>Memory management:</strong> The supplied decompressionBufferSupplier is used for
     * temporary buffers during decompression. For optimal performance:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Reuse buffers across multiple filter calls using a caching supplier</li>
     *   <li>For small batches, allocating large buffers (64KB+) may dominate decompression cost</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter determining which records to retain
     * @param destinationBuffer           The target ByteBuffer for filtered records. May be re
... (truncated)
18. EDIT /workspace/kafka--e678b4b/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    @Override
    public Iterable<MutableRecordBatch> batches() {
        return batches;
    }
After
    /**
     * Returns an iterable of all record batches in this MemoryRecords. Batches are deserialized
     * lazily as the iterable is traversed; no deserialization occurs until iteration begins.
     *
     * <p><strong>Performance note:</strong> Iterable may be traversed multiple times, with each
     * iteration creating fresh batch views of the underlying buffer.
     *
     * <p><strong>Modification safety:</strong> The returned iterable should not be used if the
     * underlying buffer is being concurrently modified.
     *
     * @return An iterable of {@link MutableRecordBatch} instances. Never null.
     */
    @Override
    public Iterable<MutableRecordBatch> batches() {
        return batches;
    }
19. WRITE /workspace/documentation.md
# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Documentation

**Repository:** github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b (Apache Kafka mirror)
**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/`
**Date:** February 2026

## Overview

This document summarizes comprehensive Javadoc enhancements added to three critical classes handling Kafka's record batch serialization: `RecordBatch`, `DefaultRecordBatch`, and `MemoryRecords`. These classes are fundamental to Kafka's binary protocol, handling the serialization, deserialization, and manipulation of record batches.

## Classes Documented

### 1. RecordBatch (Interface)

**File:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java`

#### Enhancements Made

##### Class-Level Documentation
- **Added thread-safety guarantee:** Explicitly documented that RecordBatch implementations are **not thread-safe**. Multiple threads should not access the same batch concurrently, though different batches can be safely accessed in parallel.
- **Added usage context:** Clarified that batch instances are typically accessed through `MemoryRecords` or `Records` implementations rather than directly instantiated.
- **Kafka protocol role:** Documented that batches are a fundamental container for records in both legacy (v0/v1) and modern (v2+) formats.

##### Method-Level Documentation Enhanced
- **`isValid()`** - Clarified return value semantics and CRC algorithm
- **`ensureValid()`** - Added @throws documentation for `CorruptRecordException`
- **`maxTimestamp()`** - Distinguished between CREATE_TIME and LOG_APPEND_TIME semantics; noted role of broker vs. producer timestamps
- **`baseOffset()` / `lastOffset()`** - Added performance notes; emphasized that `lastOffset()` is more efficient across all magic vers
... (truncated)
